nativeRange.habitat: Reef slopes, lagoons, and intertidal to subtidal zones in shallow tropical and subtropical waters; seldom found deeper than 15 m

decorPreferences: stable flat or angled rockwork surface

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via zooxanthellae; supplemental spot feeding with meaty foods such as mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, or prepared LPS coral foods has been reported to improve growth and coloration. Feeding tentacles are generally extended only at night. Weekly target feeding is commonly recommended.

goodWith: non-aggressive LPS corals with adequate spacing
avoidWith: corals placed within sweeper tentacle reach (5–10 cm)
highly aggressive LPS corals (e.g., Galaxea, Euphyllia without spacing)

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by ajott (1200x822). Goniastrea pectinata: whole rounded meandroid brain colony in situ on a Red Sea reef with anthias for scale; sharp, well-lit, full colony in frame.
Photo by Diego Delso (2795x2795). Goniastrea pectinata close-up: vivid orange corallites with cream shared walls showing the diagnostic honeycomb/meandroid pattern; tack-sharp macro extra.
Photo by Susan Prior (1500x1500). Paragoniastrea australensis (formerly Goniastrea australensis): striking blue valleys with golden meandroid ridges; sharp, vivid war-coral coloration.
Photo by Philippe Bourjon (1200x871). Goniastrea stelligera: whole dome colony in situ on a reef; adds species variety though coloration is muted tan.
Photo by David Witherall and Cameron Keith (640x640). Goniastrea favulus: live intertidal colony showing the meandroid brain valleys; modest resolution, included for species breadth.
Photo by elawrey (2048x1536). Coelastrea aspera (formerly Goniastrea aspera): whole live cerioid colony in situ; adds species breadth though coloration is dull.
NPS photo by Larry Basch (504x378). Goniastrea edwardsi: live cerioid dome colony in situ; low resolution and dull, included only for species coverage.

summary: The Goniastrea Coral is a large polyp stony (LPS) brain coral found across the Indo-Pacific and Red Sea, sold in the aquarium trade under names such as War Coral and Honeycomb Brain Coral. It is widely considered one of the more forgiving LPS corals for reef aquariums, tolerating a range of lighting and flow conditions.
careNotes:
TAXONOMIC NOTE: Goniastrea is a genus of roughly nine recognized species in the family Merulinidae, the stony coral family placed within the order Scleractinia. The genus was historically grouped under Faviidae, but was reclassified following molecular studies that reshuffled much of what the hobby once called 'brain corals'. In the aquarium trade, specimens are most commonly sold simply as 'Goniastrea sp.' without species-level identification. A useful field feature is that Goniastrea corallites (the individual cup-shaped skeletal units) share walls with one another — a pattern described as meandroid to cerioid — distinguishing the genus from the closely related Dipsastraea (formerly Favia), where each corallite stands separately. The trade names 'War Coral' and 'Honeycomb Brain Coral' refer to this genus, though both names are occasionally applied loosely to other brain-patterned LPS corals.

CARE OVERVIEW: Goniastrea is generally considered one of the more forgiving LPS corals for reef aquariums. Its tolerance of a range of lighting and flow conditions, combined with relatively undemanding feeding requirements, makes it broadly accessible to hobbyists who have established a stable reef system.

LIGHTING: Moderate light intensity is generally advised, roughly 75–200 PAR (a measure of the photosynthetically useful light reaching the coral). Excessively high light has been reported to cause tissue stress and color loss. LED, T5, and metal halide fixtures have all been reported to work well at appropriate intensities.

FLOW: Moderate water movement is generally recommended — sufficient to keep detritus from settling on the tissue and to deliver food particles to the polyps, without directing a strong, focused current at the colony. Direct, strong laminar flow has been reported to impede feeding and stress the tissue.

PLACEMENT: Typically placed on stable rockwork in the middle of the aquarium. The encrusting to dome-shaped colony form settles well on horizontal or gently angled surfaces. Adequate spacing from neighbors is important: sweeper tentacles — slender filaments the coral extends to sting competing organisms — have been reported to reach 5–10 cm (roughly 2–4 inches) beyond the colony margin, primarily at night. These tentacles can damage neighboring corals, so planned spacing and regular monitoring as the colony grows are both strongly advised.

AGGRESSION: Goniastrea is widely considered semi-aggressive, though some sources rate the genus as fully aggressive due to its sweeper tentacle reach. That 5–10 cm extension at night is the main concern: corals placed within range can suffer tissue damage even if they appear unaffected during the day. Careful spacing from all neighbors is strongly advised regardless of which label is applied.

WATER CHEMISTRY: Standard reef chemistry applies. Maintaining calcium, alkalinity, and magnesium within recommended reef ranges is generally advised to support sustained skeletal growth.

FEEDING: Goniastrea is primarily photosynthetic, drawing energy from symbiotic algae (zooxanthellae) living within its tissue. Supplemental spot feeding once weekly has been reported to noticeably improve growth rate and color. Small meaty foods such as mysis shrimp, frozen brine shrimp, zooplankton, or prepared LPS coral foods are generally accepted when offered during evening hours when feeding tentacles are extended.

COLONY SIZE: Wild colonies of Goniastrea retiformis have been reported to reach up to 50 cm in diameter and up to 1 m in height. Captive growth is slow; purchased frags (typically around 2–3 cm) can grow steadily into fist-sized colonies over years under good conditions.

SALINITY: Standard reef specific gravity of 1.025–1.026 is recommended. Gradual acclimation is advised for newly acquired specimens.
breedingNotes: Fragmentation is the standard captive propagation method. Goniastrea colonies can be cut with bone cutters or a bandsaw, and the resulting fragments generally encrust and grow onto frag plugs or rubble given stable water conditions. Sexual reproduction in the wild occurs via broadcast spawning, in which eggs and sperm are released into the water column simultaneously. Goniastrea favulus has been documented as a simultaneous hermaphrodite — an individual capable of producing both eggs and sperm — and has been reported to self-fertilize, which is unusual among broadcast-spawning corals. Deliberate captive sexual breeding is not practiced in the hobby.

primary.notes: Primary source for water parameters, PAR, flow, aggression, and care level. LiveAquaria (listed primary for coral-saltwater slice) consistently returned homepage redirects rather than species-specific pages and could not be used as primary. ReefChasers used as primary per fallback rule.

additional.name: Wikipedia – Goniastrea retiformis
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (Merulinidae / Scleractinia), native range confirmation, colony size (up to 50 cm diameter, up to 1 m height), and habitat (reef slopes, shallow waters). Not used for husbandry parameters.

additional.name: Wikipedia – Goniastrea favulus
additional.notes: Cross-checked native range (East Africa, Madagascar, Maldives, SE Asia, Japan, Pacific islands) and depth range (seldom deeper than 15 m). Also noted simultaneous hermaphrodite reproduction mode. Not used for husbandry parameters.

additional.name: World Wide Corals – Goniastrea search
additional.notes: Confirmed trade category as LPS and scientific name 'Goniastrea sp.' from product listings. No detailed care parameters available on product pages; general care guides page did not include Goniastrea-specific guide.
